papers.title: Integrative bioinformatics and experimental analysis identify S100A10 as a diagnostic and prognostic biomarker in pancreatic adenocarcinoma.
papers.abstract: Pancreatic adenocarcinoma (PAAD) is an extremely invasive malignancy with limited effective treatment options. S100 calcium-binding protein A10 (S100A10), a protein-coding gene, plays a critical role in tumor progression. However, its correlation with immune cell infiltration and PAAD prognosis has not been previously reported. This study aimed to systematically explore the expression pattern, biological function and clinical value of S100A10 in PAAD.
papers.summary: This study identifies S100A10 as a potential diagnostic and prognostic biomarker for pancreatic adenocarcinoma by integrating bioinformatics and experimental analyses. The authors show that S100A10 is associated with immune cell infiltration and tumor progression, suggesting it could be a target for therapy or a marker for patient prognosis.

papers.title: Crystalline silica particles exposure modulated miRNA expression profiles in human macrophage-like THP-1 cells.
papers.abstract: Occupational and environmental exposure to crystalline silica particles is a major global health concern linked to silicosis, pulmonary fibrosis, autoimmune disease, and lung cancer. MicroRNAs (miRNAs) regulate gene expression post-transcriptionally and are emerging as potential effect biomarkers of exposure in human biomonitoring studies. To identify intracellular and extracellular miRNAs responses to silica-particle exposure in human THP-1 derived macrophages, differentiated THP-1 derived macrophages were exposed to 0-300 µg/mL crystalline silica particles for 24 h. Cytotoxicity was assessed
papers.summary: This study investigates how exposure to crystalline silica particles alters microRNA (miRNA) expression in human macrophage-like cells, identifying potential biomarkers for silica-induced diseases like silicosis and lung cancer. The researchers exposed THP-1 derived macrophages to varying concentrations of silica and found dose-dependent changes in both intracellular and extracellular miRNA profiles. These findings suggest that specific miRNAs could serve as early effect biomarkers for monitoring occupational silica exposure.

papers.title: Molecular Safeguards of Survival: De novo transcriptome assembly and tissue-specific transcriptomic profiling of the yellow-foot clam Paphia malabarica.
papers.abstract: The yellow-foot clam, Paphia malabarica Chemnitz, 1782 is a regionally important fishery and aquaculture resource along the southwest coast of India. The fishery of this species from the Ashtamudi estuary has been certified as sustainable by the Marine Stewardship Council. We generated the first high-quality, comprehensive de novo transcriptome assembly of Paphia malabarica using Illumina paired-end sequencing. A total of 729.2 million raw reads were generated and further processed and assembled using the Trinity assembler. The final assembly yielded 55,980 predicted coding sequences (CDS) wit
papers.summary: This study presents the first high-quality de novo transcriptome assembly for the yellow-foot clam, a sustainably fished species in India. Using Illumina sequencing, the authors generated over 729 million reads and assembled 55,980 coding sequences, providing a valuable genomic resource for understanding the biology and conservation of this economically important bivalve.

papers.title: A single-cell transcriptomic atlas of the pigtail macaque placenta in late gestation.
papers.abstract: The placenta is a complex organ with multiple immune and non-immune cell types that promote fetal tolerance and facilitate the transfer of nutrients and oxygen. The nonhuman primate (NHP) is a key experimental model for studying human pregnancy complications, in part due to similarities in placental structure, which makes it essential to understand how single-cell populations compare across the human and NHP maternal-fetal interface. We constructed a single-cell RNA-Seq (scRNA-Seq) atlas of the placenta from the pigtail macaque (Macaca nemestrina) in the third trimester, comprising three diffe
papers.summary: This study presents a single-cell RNA sequencing atlas of the pigtail macaque placenta during late gestation, identifying 13 distinct cell types and comparing them to human placental cells. The findings reveal both conserved and species-specific gene expression patterns, highlighting the macaque as a valuable model for studying human pregnancy complications.

papers.title: Exploratory Machine Learning and Omics Integration in the Search for Biomarkers of Papillary Thyroid Cancer.
papers.abstract: Papillary thyroid carcinoma (PTC) is among the most common endocrine malignancies worldwide, and although generally associated with a favorable prognosis, a subset of patients develops aggressive disease with higher recurrence risk. This highlights the need for improved molecular characterization. Data integration approaches combined with computational methods offer new opportunities to refine diagnosis and uncover disease mechanisms. This study aims to integrate omics data and apply machine learning (ML) to identify clinically relevant biomarkers in papillary thyroid carcinoma. We selected 11
papers.summary: This study integrates multiple omics data types with machine learning to identify biomarkers that could improve diagnosis and understanding of papillary thyroid carcinoma, particularly for aggressive cases. The approach aims to refine molecular characterization beyond current clinical methods.

papers.title: Multi-omics analysis of extracellular vesicle cargo in cancer.
papers.abstract: Extracellular vesicles (EVs) are increasingly recognized as minimally invasive sources of molecular information in cancer, carrying nucleic acids, proteins, lipids, and metabolites that reflect tumor biology. Recent advances in high-throughput sequencing and mass spectrometry have not only enabled multi-omics profiling of EV cargo but also revealed that different molecular layers capture distinct yet complementary aspects of cancer processes. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the rapidly expanding landscape of EV multi-omics, highlighting methodological constraints, the predomin
papers.summary: This review discusses how extracellular vesicles (EVs) from tumors carry diverse molecular cargo—including nucleic acids, proteins, lipids, and metabolites—that can be profiled using multi-omics technologies to gain complementary insights into cancer biology. The authors highlight methodological challenges and the potential of EV multi-omics for minimally invasive cancer diagnostics.

papers.title: MIND: multimodal integration with neighbourhood-aware distributions.
papers.abstract: Multimodal data integration combines different data modalities to improve predictive and classification performance. In biology, multi-omics profiling has become a powerful tool for applications such as cancer patient stratification. However, integration of multi-omics data remains challenging because of missingness and inherent heterogeneity. Methods such as imputation and sample exclusion often rely on strong assumptions that could lead to information loss or distortion. To address these limitations, we propose MIND (Multimodal Integration with Neighbourhood-aware Distributions), which learn
papers.summary: MIND is a new computational method for integrating multi-omics data that handles missing data and biological heterogeneity without relying on strong assumptions like imputation or sample exclusion. It learns neighborhood-aware distributions to combine different data modalities, improving patient stratification in cancer and other applications.

papers.title: A pragmatist approach to bridging tables and ontologies through LinkML and punning.
papers.abstract: Ontologies support data integration and knowledge discovery across multiple life science disciplines through the application of formal semantics that define hierarchical and cross-hierarchical relationships which enable automated reasoning capabilities. At the same time, tabular data is by far the most commonly produced format of data in most disciplines. This is especially true in biodiversity dataset production. Creating rich semantic data from tables can be complex because of a mismatch between flat tabular data and hierarchical ontology models. Here, we present a practical approach for clo
papers.summary: This paper presents a practical method using LinkML and punning to bridge the gap between flat tabular data and hierarchical ontology models, enabling richer semantic data creation from tables. The approach addresses the common mismatch in biodiversity and other life science datasets, making it easier to integrate tabular data with formal ontologies for automated reasoning.

papers.title: Spatial multi-omics imputation and embedding with SpaMIE.
papers.abstract: SpaMIE is a deep graph neural network framework designed to tackle the challenge of multi-section integration in spatial multi-omics (SMO) datasets with systematic missing modalities. Current SMO platforms face limitations such as high cost and limited throughput, leading to many large-scale spatial atlases relying on cost-effective mono-omics measurements while only a few sections are profiled with full multi-omics technologies. This results in heterogeneous modality coverage across tissue sections. SpaMIE offers a two-stage solution. In the first stage, it performs spatially informed cross-m
papers.summary: SpaMIE is a new deep learning framework that uses graph neural networks to impute missing data modalities and integrate multiple tissue sections in spatial multi-omics studies, addressing the common problem of incomplete modality coverage due to cost constraints. By enabling the combination of cost-effective mono-omics measurements with a few fully profiled multi-omics sections, it allows researchers to build more comprehensive spatial atlases without requiring full multi-omics profiling for every sample.

papers.title: AMIUgraph: analysis and modeling of interactions for utility-driven benchmarking of graph-based models in healthcare.
papers.abstract: Graph-based machine learning approaches, including Knowledge Graph Embedding (KGE) methods and Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), have emerged as powerful tools for modeling complex biomedical data. However, a systematic and clinically grounded comparison of these approaches across heterogeneous healthcare graphs, accounting for both predictive performance and real-world deployment constraints, is still lacking.
papers.journal: BMC medical informatics and decision making
papers.summary: This paper introduces AMIUgraph, a framework for benchmarking graph-based machine learning models in healthcare that evaluates both predictive performance and practical deployment constraints. The authors systematically compare Knowledge Graph Embedding methods and Graph Neural Networks across heterogeneous healthcare graphs, providing a clinically grounded assessment of their utility.

papers.title: ViBioChain: a blockchain-enabled architecture for privacy-preserving, ethically governed, and explainable personalized gene editing.
papers.abstract: Personalized gene editing demands robust mechanisms for privacy, ethical governance, and verifiable data integrity. This paper proposes ViBioChain, a modular blockchain-anchored architecture integrating five components: (1) differential chain-of-custody audit combining quantum fingerprinting with post-quantum signatures for immutable genomic audit trails; (2) proof-of-bioethical-compliance employing zero-knowledge proofs and AI-based ontology evaluation for automated bioethical gating; (3) federated genomic trust mesh (FGTM) enabling privacy-preserving collaborative model training with Renyi d
papers.summary: ViBioChain proposes a blockchain-based architecture for personalized gene editing that addresses privacy, ethical governance, and data integrity through quantum fingerprinting, zero-knowledge proofs, and federated learning. The system includes automated bioethical compliance checks and privacy-preserving collaborative model training, aiming to make gene editing more secure and ethically accountable.

papers.title: LivestockDev: A multi-omics resource for exploring cross-species comparison of livestock embryogenesis.
papers.abstract: Advances in experimental and sequencing technologies have driven a surge in multi-omics data on early embryonic development in livestock species. Nevertheless, the absence of systematic data curation and standardized analytical frameworks has hindered research on early embryogenesis in livestock, limiting data integration, cross-species comparisons, and mechanistic insights. Here, we developed LivestockDev, an integrative multi-omics database dedicated to livestock embryogenesis. LivestockDev covers five major livestock species (cattle, sheep, goats, pigs, and horses) and systematically integr
papers.summary: LivestockDev is a new multi-omics database that curates and standardizes data on early embryonic development across five major livestock species (cattle, sheep, goats, pigs, and horses). By enabling systematic data integration and cross-species comparisons, this resource helps researchers overcome previous barriers to studying livestock embryogenesis and uncovering underlying mechanisms.

papers.title: Mapping ovarian cellular and molecular landscape across the lifespan of women: a scoping review.
papers.abstract: With growing interest in ART, fertility preservation, and postmenopausal health of women, reproductive medicine is increasingly focused on characterizing oocytes and ovarian tissue composition, as well as understanding the molecular mechanisms that guide ovarian function throughout its lifecycle. High-throughput omics technologies have enabled the characterization of different molecular layers, leading to substantial advances in our understanding of their complex dynamics. However, not all molecular aspects are studied equally, and studies examining the same modalities often show inconsistenci
papers.summary: This scoping review maps the cellular and molecular landscape of the human ovary across a woman's lifespan, highlighting how high-throughput omics technologies have advanced our understanding of ovarian function, oocyte quality, and tissue composition. The authors identify significant gaps and inconsistencies in the literature, particularly in the uneven study of different molecular modalities and the need for more integrated, systematic approaches.

papers.title: FAIR Data Standards for AI in Plant Biology: Current Practice and Case Studies.
papers.abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) has become a central analytical approach in plant science, supporting prediction, pattern discovery, and integration across molecular, phenotypic, and environmental data. While methodological advances have been rapid, progress is increasingly constrained less by algorithms than by the structure, semantics, and interoperability of the underlying data. In this review, we examine how the Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable (FAIR) principles are currently realized in AI-driven plant research and how different implementations shape which AI analyses are fe
papers.summary: This review examines how FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) data principles are applied in AI-driven plant biology research, arguing that data structure and interoperability now constrain progress more than algorithmic advances. The authors analyze current practices and case studies to show how different implementations of FAIR principles shape the types of AI analyses that are feasible in plant science.

papers.title: Ontology-driven energy-efficient SAID framework for 5G and IoT networks using cryptographic techniques.
papers.abstract: The rapid deployment and usage of 5G and IoT networks in smart cities present significant challenges for cybersecurity, particularly regarding energy efficiency and attack detection. Existing intrusion detection systems often fail to balance high detection accuracy with low energy consumption, especially in resource-constrained environments. This research paper proposes an ontology-driven novel computational framework for energy-efficient Security Attack Identification and Detection (SAID) in 5G and IoT networks. The research framework proposed is a combination of cryptographic algorithms and
papers.summary: This paper proposes an ontology-driven framework that combines cryptographic techniques to improve energy-efficient security attack detection in 5G and IoT networks, addressing the trade-off between detection accuracy and energy consumption.

papers.title: Bioinformatics analysis identifies a novel 4-gene signature associated with immune cell infiltration in osteoarthritis: a combined bioinformatics and experimental validation study.
papers.abstract: Osteoarthritis is the most common degenerative joint condition, yet its pathogenesis remains incompletely understood. This study aims to identify key differentially expressed genes (DEGs) in osteoarthritis-affected synovial tissues and investigate potential underlying immune mechanisms using bioinformatics approaches. Gene expression datasets GSE12021, GSE55235, and GSE55457 from the Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) were analyzed, comprising 30 osteoarthritis and 28 normal synovial tissue samples. Differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were identified using the limma R package. Functional enrichme
papers.summary: This study analyzed gene expression data from osteoarthritis patients to identify a 4-gene signature linked to immune cell infiltration in synovial tissue. The findings suggest specific genes and immune mechanisms may play a role in osteoarthritis pathogenesis, though the work is largely computational with limited experimental validation.

papers.title: VITAGRAPH: building a knowledge graph for biologically relevant learning tasks.
papers.abstract: The complexity of human biology poses ongoing challenges, driving global interdisciplinary research. Artificial intelligence has become a powerful tool in computational biology, where graph data structures model entities like protein-protein interaction (PPI) networks and gene functional networks. These networks support crucial tasks in network medicine, including gene-disease association prediction, drug repurposing, and polypharmacy side-effect analysis. Reliable machine learning predictions require high-quality data. We present VITAGRAPH, a comprehensive multi-purpose biological knowledge g
papers.summary: VITAGRAPH is a new comprehensive biological knowledge graph that integrates diverse data types to support machine learning tasks in network medicine, such as predicting gene-disease associations and drug repurposing. The resource aims to provide high-quality, structured data to improve the reliability of AI-driven predictions in computational biology.

papers.title: tmQM-RDF Data Set: A Knowledge Graph Representing Transition Metal Complexes.
papers.abstract: Transition metal complexes (TMCs) have wide-ranging practical utility in chemistry, with possible applications that range from catalysis to medicinal chemistry. The study of TMCs and their properties is thus a field rich with potential, one in which machine learning and computational approaches can offer a substantial aid. For this reason, appropriate and accessible data sets, collecting a wide range of information, are required in order to facilitate the effective analysis and investigation of such compounds. This paper contributes to the data modeling effort via the introduction of the trans
papers.journal: Journal of chemical information and modeling
papers.summary: This paper introduces tmQM-RDF, a knowledge graph dataset that organizes information about transition metal complexes (TMCs) in a structured, machine-readable format. The resource aims to facilitate machine learning and computational analysis of TMCs for applications in catalysis and medicinal chemistry.

papers.title: KG-Microbe - Building Modular and Scalable Knowledge Graphs for Microbiome and Microbial Sciences.
papers.abstract: The integration of many disparate forms of data is essential for understanding the microbial world and its interaction with the environment and human health. Doing so is particularly challenging in the context of microbe-host and microbe-microbe interactions that contribute to health or environmental outcomes. There are thousands of relevant microbial species, and millions of interactions among those microbes and with their environment or host. Integrated information (e.g., about host and microbial physiology, genetics, and metabolism) facilitates deeper understanding of complex mechanisms and
papers.summary: KG-Microbe is a new framework for building modular and scalable knowledge graphs that integrate diverse microbial data, including host-microbe and microbe-microbe interactions. This tool addresses the challenge of handling millions of interactions across thousands of microbial species by enabling structured, scalable data integration. It facilitates deeper understanding of complex mechanisms in microbiome science and its links to health and the environment.

papers.title: Multimodal temporal feature fusion for teacher competency assessment and precision training resource recommendation.
papers.abstract: Assessing teacher competency in a reliable and multidimensional manner remains an open problem, largely because conventional evaluation instruments capture only a fraction of the behavioral repertoire that defines effective instruction. We tackle this challenge by developing an integrated framework that fuses heterogeneous classroom signals-video, audio, transcribed text, and physiological recordings-through modality-specific encoders coupled with a cross-modal attention mechanism. The attention module adaptively re-weights each data stream according to its diagnostic relevance for a given com
papers.summary: This paper presents a multimodal AI framework that fuses video, audio, text, and physiological data from classrooms to assess teacher competency more comprehensively than traditional methods. A cross-modal attention mechanism dynamically weights each data stream based on its diagnostic relevance, enabling more reliable and multidimensional evaluation.

papers.title: Multimodal genomic prediction is not a buzzword: why modern plant breeding must integrate genomics, enviromics, and phenomics.
papers.abstract: Plant breeding has entered a new era, transitioning from a predominantly unimodal, genomics-focused paradigm to one defined by multimodal data integration. Dense genomic markers, long-term environmental datasets, physiological and omics measurements, and high-throughput phenotyping platforms are now routine components of modern breeding programs. Paradoxically, while plant breeding now operates in a multimodal data ecosystem, most prediction models remain effectively unimodal, relying on a single type of information at a time-genomics alone, enviromics alone, physiology alone, or phenomics alo
papers.summary: This perspective argues that plant breeding has entered a multimodal data era with genomics, enviromics, and phenomics data routinely collected, yet most prediction models still rely on a single data type. The authors advocate for integrating these diverse data streams into unified prediction frameworks to fully leverage modern breeding program capabilities.

papers.title: Developing a Specialized Dravet Syndrome Ontology for Rare Disease Informatics and AI Applications.
papers.abstract: Dravet syndrome (DS) is a severe developmental and epileptic encephalopathy whose clinical and research representation requires integration of heterogeneous knowledge spanning seizures, development, behavior, SUDEP/autonomic risk, genetics, comorbidities, electrophysiology, pharmacology, and drug responsiveness. We report the development of a DS-focused ontology created by expert-guided specialization of a previously published epilepsy ontology. Scope expansion was defined through a scientific advisory board, structured review meetings, and iterative ontology curation in OWL. The resulting res
papers.journal: medRxiv : the preprint server for health sciences
papers.summary: Researchers developed a specialized ontology for Dravet syndrome by expanding an existing epilepsy ontology to cover seizures, development, genetics, and drug response. This structured knowledge representation aims to improve rare disease informatics and support AI applications.

papers.title: HeartBioPortal 3.0: an integrated cardiovascular genomics knowledge environment for molecular, clinical and population-scale interpretation.
papers.abstract: Cardiovascular genomics is producing rapidly expanding genetic, molecular, phenotypic and clinical data, yet relevant evidence remains fragmented across resources and difficult to translate into actionable biological and ultimately translational knowledge. HeartBioPortal (HBP) is a browser-based cardiovascular knowledge environment that was developed to address this problem by organizing omics, variant, phenotype and clinical evidence centered around gene queries. Here we describe HBP 3.0, a major update that expands both the data architecture and interpretive interface. This update introduces
papers.journal: medRxiv : the preprint server for health sciences
papers.summary: HeartBioPortal 3.0 is an updated web-based platform that integrates diverse cardiovascular genomics data—including genetic variants, molecular profiles, and clinical information—into a unified, gene-centered knowledge environment. This major update enhances both the underlying data architecture and the user interface, making it easier for researchers and clinicians to interpret genomic evidence in the context of cardiovascular disease.

papers.title: RD-OMICS: An Integrative Multi-Omics Data Inventory in Rare Diseases.
papers.abstract: Rare diseases (RD) impact over 30 million individuals in the United States, yet fewer than 5% of the identified conditions have FDA-approved treatments. Progress in RD research is hindered by small patient cohorts, biological heterogeneity, and the fragmented, inconsistently annotated publicly available omics data, which limits integrative analysis and translational discovery. Here, we present RD-OMICS, a data inventory with integrated and structured RD omics data from Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO), in the form of a knowledge graph. We developed a metadata harmonization pipeline that combines
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: RD-OMICS is a new knowledge graph that integrates and harmonizes multi-omics data from rare diseases, addressing the challenge of fragmented and inconsistently annotated public datasets. By structuring data from GEO, it aims to facilitate integrative analysis and translational discovery for rare diseases, where fewer than 5% have FDA-approved treatments.

papers.title: A knowledge graph and multi-agent reinforcement learning model for psychological risk identification and intervention in online learning.
papers.abstract: As the scale of online learning expanded, students tended to exhibit declining learning behaviors and accumulating task backlogs during self-directed study. These issues further led to reduced learning motivation and increased psychological pressure. To address this, this study constructed an innovative model that integrated a knowledge graph with multi-agent reinforcement learning. The model enabled learning state risk identification and personalized intervention. The study conducted a systematic evaluation using comprehensive learning behavior data from seven selected courses. The results in
papers.summary: This study developed a model combining knowledge graphs with multi-agent reinforcement learning to identify psychological risks in online learners and provide personalized interventions. Using data from seven courses, the model effectively detected declining learning behaviors and reduced motivation, offering a new approach to supporting student mental health in online education.

papers.title: Eras of bioinformatics technologies from command-line interfaces to artificial intelligence (AI) chatbots.
papers.abstract: Over the past 75 years, especially the recent quarter century, bioinformatics has undergone a profound transformation, evolving from a specialized field of command-line tools to a cornerstone of modern biomedical and life sciences. We trace this journey through distinct technological eras driven by exponential biological data growth and parallel computational advances. The genomic revolution established foundational sequence analysis tools and was rapidly followed by the next-generation sequencing era, when unprecedented data volumes shifted the bottleneck from generation to analysis. This dro
papers.summary: This review traces the evolution of bioinformatics over 75 years, from command-line tools to AI chatbots, highlighting how exponential biological data growth and computational advances have driven distinct technological eras. It emphasizes the shift from data generation to analysis as a key bottleneck, particularly during the next-generation sequencing era.

papers.title: Integrative proteogenomic analyses identify plasma proteins that impact the risk of ischemic stroke.
papers.abstract: Understanding the proteins implicated in the pathogenesis of ischemic stroke is important for elucidating disease mechanisms and informing prevention strategies. In this study, we aim to identify plasma proteins with a potentially causal effect on risk of ischemic stroke by integrating the largest available genetic data sources for plasma proteins, ischemic stroke and its risk factors.
papers.summary: This study integrates large-scale genetic data to identify plasma proteins that may causally influence the risk of ischemic stroke, providing insights into disease mechanisms and potential prevention targets. By combining genetic data on plasma proteins, ischemic stroke, and its risk factors, the authors pinpoint specific proteins with potential causal roles.

papers.title: MetagenomicKG: a knowledge graph for metagenomic applications.
papers.abstract: The sheer volume and variety of genomic content within microbial communities makes metagenomics a field rich in biomedical knowledge. To traverse these complex communities and their vast unknowns, metagenomic studies often depend on distinct reference databases, such as the Genome Taxonomy Database (GTDB), the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG), and the Bacterial and Viral Bioinformatics Resource Center (BV-BRC), for various analytical purposes. These databases are crucial for the genetic and functional annotation of microbial communities. Nevertheless, the inconsistent nomenclatur
papers.summary: The paper introduces MetagenomicKG, a knowledge graph that integrates multiple reference databases (GTDB, KEGG, BV-BRC) to unify nomenclature and facilitate exploration of microbial communities. This resource aims to streamline metagenomic analyses by linking genetic, taxonomic, and functional data in a structured, queryable format.

papers.title: AART enables fast and accurate cross-platform proteomic translation.
papers.abstract: Plasma proteomic profiling has been widely used for biomarker discovery, disease prediction and diagnosis, and patient stratification. However, technical differences across assay platforms often result in low-to-moderate agreement, limiting study reproducibility, data integration, and model transferability. Here we present AART, a cross-platform proteomic translation framework that integrates matched-protein ridge regression with proteome-wide residual learning. We benchmarked AART spanning three independent cohorts profiled using three major platforms, including Olink, SomaScan, and mass spec
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: AART is a new computational framework that translates protein measurements across different assay platforms (Olink, SomaScan, mass spec) with high accuracy, overcoming the low agreement that currently hinders data integration and reproducibility in plasma proteomics.

papers.title: GlyGen: a knowledgebase linking glycan data with protein and gene data to reveal novel biological connections.
papers.abstract: Glycans influence protein structure and function, modulate tissue development, regulate organ function, drive pathogen interactions, control inflammation and immunity, and impact most disease processes. However, glycan and glycosylation data are frequently difficult to access, existing across heterogeneous resources, with inconsistent representation and limited linkage to the genes, proteins, and protein sites that define their biological context. This fragmentation limits integration with genomics, proteomics, and other omics data, and constrains the discovery of glycan-mediated relationships
papers.summary: GlyGen is a new knowledgebase that integrates hard-to-access glycan data with related protein and gene information, using consistent formats to enable researchers to discover biological connections involving glycans. By linking glycan structures to their genetic and protein context, it overcomes the fragmentation that previously limited integration with other omics data and constrained discovery of glycan-mediated relationships.

papers.title: Artificial intelligence optimization model for public safety in smart cities based on multi-source data collaboration in the internet of things.
papers.abstract: The prevalent cross-departmental data barriers and ineffective public safety risk management and control in smart city construction severely restrict governance efficiency. This study takes Hefei as an empirical case and innovatively proposes and validates a "technology-institution-data" ternary collaborative smart city public safety artificial intelligence optimization model. The core contribution lies in constructing a three-layer conceptual framework of "perception-decision-value", systematically integrating real-time perception driven by the Internet of Things (IoT), intelligent decision-m
papers.summary: This paper proposes an AI optimization model for public safety in smart cities that integrates IoT-driven real-time perception with intelligent decision-making, using Hefei as a case study. The model addresses cross-departmental data barriers through a 'technology-institution-data' framework, aiming to improve governance efficiency. The work is primarily an applied systems engineering study rather than a bioinformatics contribution.

papers.title: Mechanistically Informed and Omics-Guided Essential Oil Applications for Food Preservation.
papers.abstract: The growing demand for safe, sustainable, and clean-label food preservation strategies has accelerated interest in plant-derived essential oils (EOs) as natural antimicrobial agents. These chemically complex mixtures exhibit broad-spectrum activity against foodborne pathogens and spoilage microorganisms through multitarget mechanisms, including membrane disruption, metabolic interference, oxidative stress induction, and modulation of quorum sensing and biofilm formation. However, their practical application remains constrained by compositional variability, physicochemical instability, matrix-d
papers.summary: This review discusses how essential oils can be used as natural antimicrobials for food preservation, focusing on their multi-target mechanisms like membrane disruption and biofilm inhibition. It highlights the challenges of compositional variability and instability that limit practical use.

papers.title: Targeting mir130b-IL-33-PD-L1 axis effectively inhibits esophageal squamous carcinoma progression.
papers.abstract: We used an integrated approach combining molecular biology techniques, animal experiments, and clinical samples to elucidate the mechanistic basis of interleukin-33 (IL-33)-induced programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1) upregulation and its role in squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC) progression. Multi-omics analyses and clinical datasets identified that increased expression of IL-33 and its receptor in tumor tissues correlated with advanced stage and poor prognosis. IL-33 expression was associated with enhanced migration, invasion, and proliferation of tumor cells, and these effects were partly reverse
papers.summary: This study identifies a new regulatory axis (mir130b-IL-33-PD-L1) that drives esophageal squamous cell carcinoma progression by upregulating PD-L1. Using multi-omics and clinical data, the authors show that IL-33 and its receptor are elevated in tumors and linked to poor prognosis, and that targeting this axis can reverse tumor-promoting effects. The findings suggest a potential therapeutic strategy for ESCC by disrupting this immune checkpoint-related pathway.

papers.title: Circulating multi-omic signature of disease severity and cholangiocarcinoma in primary sclerosing cholangitis.
papers.abstract: Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) is a chronic cholestatic liver disease often associated with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) that can progress to cirrhosis or cholangiocarcinoma (CCA), both carrying a poor prognosis. The mechanisms driving disease progression remain poorly understood. We aimed to identify circulating multi-omic signatures linked to PSC severity (alkaline phosphatase, bilirubin, fibrosis), IBD, and CCA to elucidate underlying biology and discover potential biomarkers for risk stratification and disease monitoring.
papers.summary: This study identifies circulating multi-omic signatures associated with disease severity and cholangiocarcinoma in primary sclerosing cholangitis, potentially revealing underlying biological mechanisms and biomarkers for risk stratification.

papers.title: Enhancing medical Q&A systems with multimodal knowledge graphs and dual-layer attention mechanisms.
papers.abstract: Medical intelligent question-answering (QA) systems have become important tools for improving the efficiency of healthcare services, and recent research has increasingly emphasized performance optimization and multimodal integration. However, existing systems still face several challenges in intent recognition, entity extraction, and multimodal knowledge fusion, particularly reduced accuracy in multi-label classification, heavy reliance on large-scale annotated data, and limited support for cross-modal retrieval. To address these issues, this study proposes a medical intelligent QA framework t
papers.summary: This paper proposes a new medical question-answering framework that uses multimodal knowledge graphs and dual-layer attention mechanisms to improve intent recognition, entity extraction, and cross-modal retrieval. The approach aims to overcome limitations in existing systems, such as reduced accuracy in multi-label classification and heavy reliance on annotated data.

papers.title: Microbial Ecology at the Nexus of Food Safety and Biotechnology With Ecological Mechanisms, Risks, and Emerging Innovations.
papers.abstract: Food systems are complex microbial ecosystems in which microorganisms play dual and often contrasting roles as agents of foodborne contamination and as essential drivers of food production and biotechnological innovation. Microbial ecology provides an integrative framework for understanding how microbial interactions, environmental conditions, and human interventions shape food safety outcomes and technological processes. This narrative integrative review is aimed at synthesizing current literature on microbial ecology at the nexus of food safety and food biotechnology and at identifying key r
papers.summary: This review explores how microbial ecology connects food safety and biotechnology, highlighting the dual role of microorganisms as both contaminants and beneficial agents in food production. It synthesizes current literature on microbial interactions and environmental factors that influence food safety and biotechnological processes.
